coredata.dc:title: Polyamidoamine dendrimers-capped carbon dots/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites and its application for electrochemical immunosensor
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Qi
Han, Jingman
Ma, Zhanfang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, polyamidoamine dendrimers capped-carbon dots (PAMAM-CDs) were fabricated by one-step microwave assisted pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and PAMAM, where the formation of CDs and the surface passivation were accomplished simultaneously. The obtained graphitic PAMAM-CDs, with abundant amine groups, were employed as reducing and capping agents for the formation of PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites. The resulting nanocomposites exhibited excellent conductivity, stability and biocompatibility on the surface of electrode and were designed as an immobilized matrix for sensitive immunosensing of alpha-fetoprotein (AFP). The proposed immunosensor showed a wide linear detection range from 100fgmL−1 to 100ngmL−1. The detection limit for AFP was 0.025pgmL−1. Importantly, the immunosensor was evaluated for the analysis of clinical serum samples, obtaining a good correlation with enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). The results indicated that the immunosensor provided a possible application for the detection of AFP in clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyamidoamine dendrimers
Carbon dots
Au nanocrystal
Label-free electrochemical immunosensor
Alpha-fetoprotein

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites
Fabrication and characterization of the immunosensor
Optimization of the experimental parameters of the immunosensor
Selectivity, repeatability, and stability of the immunosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of the PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites (A); and fabrication of the PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites-based electrochemical immunosensor (B).
HRTEM images of PAMAM-CDs (A). TEM image of PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites (B), inset shows the corresponding HRTEM image.

DPV (A) and EIS (B) of each surface modification step performed in 5mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− containing 0.1M KCl. Bare GCE (a); PAMAM-CDs/Au modified electrode (b); anti-AFP/PAMAM-CDs/Au modified electrode (c); anti-AFP/PAMAM-CDs/Au modified electrode blocked with BSA (d); and modified GCE after incubation with 1ngmL−1 AFP (e).
Calibration plot of current against log AFP concentration for the immunosensor under the optimization conditions (A). The selectivity of the immunosensor (B).
Assay results of clinical serum samples using the proposed and reference methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values shown here are the average values from three measurements.

head.ce:title: Polyamidoamine dendrimers-capped carbon dots/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites and its application for electrochemical immunosensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University, Beijing 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, polyamidoamine dendrimers capped-carbon dots (PAMAM-CDs) were fabricated by one-step microwave assisted pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and PAMAM, where the formation of CDs and the surface passivation were accomplished simultaneously. The obtained graphitic PAMAM-CDs, with abundant amine groups, were employed as reducing and capping agents for the formation of PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites. The resulting nanocomposites exhibited excellent conductivity, stability and biocompatibility on the surface of electrode and were designed as an immobilized matrix for sensitive immunosensing of alpha-fetoprotein (AFP). The proposed immunosensor showed a wide linear detection range from 100fgmL−1 to 100ngmL−1. The detection limit for AFP was 0.025pgmL−1. Importantly, the immunosensor was evaluated for the analysis of clinical serum samples, obtaining a good correlation with enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). The results indicated that the immunosensor provided a possible application for the detection of AFP in clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer biomarkers are molecules occurring in blood or tissue, which are associated with cancer. The early detection of cancer biomarker is of great importance in proper prevention, diagnosis and treatment of cancer (Chen et al., 2013; Li et al., 2012a,b; Wang et al., 2011). α-fetoprotein (AFP) is one of the most extensively used clinical cancer biomarker (Wang and Xie, 1999). Elevated AFP concentration in serum may be an early indication of some cancerous diseases such as hepatic carcinoma, nasopharyngeal cancer and epithelial ovarian tumors (Huang et al., 2012). Therefore, the detection of AFP is absolutely necessary in clinical assay.

Among various immunoassay methods, electrochemical immunoassay is one of the predominant analytical methods due to its high sensitivity and selectivity, good compatibility and wide liner range (Song et al., 2011; Sun and Ma, 2012; Liu and Ma, 2013). During the fabrication of electrochemical immunosensor, the stability and activity of the immunological reagents on the electrode are key factors to dramatically enhance the signal intensity. Recently, carbon materials, including graphene (Li et al., 2013; Chen et al., 2012; Han et al., 2013), carbon nanotube (Lin et al., 2013; Lai et al., 2013) and carbon sphere (Cui and Zhu, 2010; Chen et al., 2010), have been extensively employed in analytical applications, especially for electrochemical immunosensor. They have been used for electrode modification and biomolecule immobilization attribute to their high electrical conductivity, chemical stability and multifunctional structure. Nevertheless, the carbon materials face the problems of complicated synthesis process, hydrophobicity, poor dispersion and solubility (Edwards and Coleman, 2013; Tang et al., 2009). Consequently, it is still a challenge to explore novel nanomaterials for improving the sensing interface on the electrode.

Traditional semiconductor quantum dots (such as CdSe, CdTe) are commonly synthesized using toxic heavy metals, which limit their widespread use and in biological application because of potential environmental hazard and long term toxicity concerns (Shen et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2012a,b,c). Compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots or metal quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of carbon nonomaterials, recently have attracted considerable attention due to their high aqueous solubility, favorable optical properties, excellent electron donors and acceptors, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility (Li et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2012; Song et al., 2012).

Until now, CDs have found potential applications in bioimaging (Goh et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2012a,b,c), photocatalysis (Cao et al., 2011; Li et al., 2012a,b), optoelectronics (Mirtchew et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2011), chemiluminescence (Lin et al., 2010; Dong et al., 2010), ion/small molecule detection (Zhou et al., 2012; Yu et al., 2013). Compared with other carbon materials, the CDs possess many advantages such as high surface area and reactivity (Dai et al., 2012). Notably, unlike nanodiamonds, CDs are conjugated system which have greater sp2 character (Li et al., 2012a,b), which can accelerate the direct electron transfer. Moreover, CDs also offer the possibility of combining other types of nanomaterials to form nanocomposites, merging different properties in a single new material, such as CDs/Ag3PO4 for the photocatalysis of organic compounds (Zhang et al., 2012a,b,c); CDs/Pt for the CO2 conversion (Cao et al., 2011) and CDs/Nation for the electrochemiluminescence immunosensor (Dai et al., 2012). Owing to the advantageous physicochemical properties, CDs proved to be an exciting building block candidate for biomolecules immobilization and biosensor fabrication.

Herein, polyamidoamine dendrimers capped-carbon dots (PAMAM-CDs) were fabricated by one-step microwave assisted pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and PAMAM, where the formation of CDs and the surface passivation were accomplished simultaneously. PAMAM dendrimers are highly branched and monodispersed macromolecules with well-defined composition and construction (Deng et al., 2005). They have been widely used as templates for the preparation of inorganic nanoparticles (Huang et al., 2012; Krasteva et al., 2002). The obtained graphitic PAMAM-CDs, with abundant amine groups, were employed as reducing and capping agents for the formation of PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites. The amine-rich PAMAM played two key roles in this system: passivating the surface of CDs and participating in the reduction of HAuCl4. The PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites were successfully used to fabricate a simple, label-free and ultrasensitive electrochemical immunosensor for AFP. The main role of CDs in this immunosensor is to accelerate the electron transfer process between sensing interface and the electrode. Besides, PAMAM-capped CDs could also enhance the stability of PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites on the electrode through electrostatic interaction and the film-forming property of PAMAM (Shen and Hu, 2004). At the same time, Au nanocrystal that was not coated by PAMAM-CDs could offer a large specific surface area and active sites to immobilize antibody. This immunosensor showed high sensitivity, good repeatability, and wide dynamic response for AFP. Moreover, the immunosensor was also evaluated for the analysis of clinical serum samples.

α-fetoprotein antigen (AFP) and α-fetoprotein monoclonal antibody (anti-AFP) were purchased from Linc-Bio Company (Shanghai, China). Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) was purchased from Biosynthesis Biotechnology Company (Beijing, China). Human immunoglobulin G (IgG) was purchased from Chengwen Biological Company (Beijing, China). Polyamidoamine dendrimers (PAMAM, G=2.0) was obtained from Aldrich. Hydrogen Tetrachloroaurate hydrate (HAuCl4·xH2O, 99%), d-(+)-Glucose, ascorbic acid (AA) were achieved from Alfa Aesar. Urea acid (UA), albumin from bovine serum (BSA), NaH2PO4, NaHPO4, KCl, K3Fe(CN)6 and K4Fe(CN)6 were obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagents Company (Beijing, China). The clinical human samples were from the Capital Normal University Hospital. Ultrapure water (resistivity >18MΩcm−2) was used throughout the experiments.

The synthesis of PAMAM-CDs was conducted by microwave reaction instrument (CEM, American). UV–vis spectroscopy was characterized by a UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed with a JEOL-100CX electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan) under 80kV accelerating voltage. High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were obtained on a JEOL-2011 electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a VECTORTM 22 spectrometer (Bruker) with KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of the samples was performed with an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (Thermofisher, American). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) spectroscopy were taken on a Hitachi S-4800 SEM. Fluorescence spectrum was obtained by a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured with the advanced Electrochemical system of a PARSTAT 2273 (Princeton Co., USA). Electrochemical measurements were carried out on CHI-832 electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, China). A conventional three-electrode system was used for the electrolytic system, including a glass carbon electrode (GCE, ∅=3mm) as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode (sat. KCl) as the reference electrode.

The PAMAM-CDs were synthesized by a facile green route of microwave assisted pyrolysis method. In a typical experiment, 2mL PAMAM (1molL−1) and 20mL phosphate buffer solution (0.1molL−1) were mixed with 1g citric acid under vigorous stirring to form a homogeneous solution in a common 50mL beaker. The mixture was then reacted in microwave reaction instrument (250W) at 170°C for 20min, and then cooled down to room temperature. The resulting product was dialyzed against water using a cellulose ester dialysis membrane bag (molecular weight cutoff (MWCO)=3500) for three days to remove excess CA and PAMAM. The purified PAMAM-CDs were then concentrated using a rotary evaporator to 10mL before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: 200μL aqueous solution of HAuCl4 (2molL−1) was added to 3mL aqueous suspension of the obtained PAMAM-CDs. The mixed solution was kept at 100°C with stirring for 2h to yield a stable purple suspension. The resultant was centrifuged at 13000rpm for 20min and redispersed in 400μL water. The product was stored under 4°C before use.

Prior to experiment, the GCE was carefully polished with 1.0, 0.3 and 0.05μm alumina powder separately, followed by successive sonication in deionized water and ethanol for 5min and dried in nitrogen.

5μL PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites solution was cast onto the electrode and dried in air. Then the obtained electrode was incubated with anti-AFP (200μgmL−1, in 0.1molL−1 phosphate buffer, and pH 7.3) at 4°C overnight. Finally, the anti-AFP modified electrode was incubated in a solution of BSA (1.0wt%) for 1h at room temperature to block possible remaining active sites and avoid non-specific adsorption. After each step, the electrode was thoroughly washed with 0.1M PBS (pH 7.3) to remove physically absorbed species. The proposed immunosensor was stored at 4°C when not in use.

All electrochemical measurements were carried out in 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH 7.3), containing 5mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3−. The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) scan was taken from −0.2V to 0.6V (vs SCE). When measuring the detection, the as-prepared electrode was incubated in various concentrations of AFP for 30min at 37°C, and then washed by PBS.

Typical UV/Vis absorption spectrum of PAMAM-CDs was shown in Fig. S1, the PAMAM-CDs solution has two absorption bands at 270nm and 374nm, respectively. It has been reported that peak at 250–300nm represents a typical absorption of an aromatic system, which is similar to that of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (Xie et al., 1999). As shown in Fig. 1A, the PAMAM-CDs were monodisperse nanocrystals with an average diameter of around 2.0nm. The HRTEM image (inset in Fig. 1A) clearly reveals a lattice spacing of 0.212nm, which is consistent with the (100) facet of graphite (Dong et al., 2012). These results indicated that the obtained PAMAM-CDs have a graphitic structure. To confirm the coating of PAMAM on the surface of CDs, FTIR spectrum was recorded to identify the organic functional groups on PAMAM-CDs (Fig. S2). The strong peaks of 3407cm−1 and 3132cm−1 were attributed to the (–NH–), the peaks at 1710cm−1 and 1612cm−1 corresponded to amide linkage (–CONH–), peaks at 2955cm−1, 2850cm−1 and 1400cm−1 were identified as (−–CH2−), indicating the successful attachment of PAMAM on the surface of CDs. XPS results showed that the as-prepared PAMAM-CDs contain mainly carbon, nitrogen and oxygen (Fig. S4A). The N 1s peak at 400.2eV was observed, which was attributed to the amino groups (NH2) (Liu et al., 2010). The C 1s deconvolution spectrum of PAMAM-CDs revealed the presence of three main components of carbons：C–C (284.8eV), C–N (286.3eV) and C=O (288.2eV) (Fig. S4B). Among the three peaks, the C–C sp2 appearing at 284.8eV also suggested the graphite structure of PAMAM-CDs. The C–N at 286.3eV could be implied as the coating of PAMAM. Apparently, the above characterization datas indicated that the obtained CDs were PAMAM-capped graphite nanocrystals.

Fig. 1B shows the typical TEM image of PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites, revealing that the nanocomposites were nearly spherical in shape with diameters of around 15nm. The SEM image of PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites was shown in Fig. S5A. It can be found that the products mainly consist of uniform nanoparticles with size ranging from 15 to 20nm. To confirm the formation of PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites, HRTEM image was recorded (inset in Fig. 1B). The interplanar spacing of 0.212nm and 0.24nm were clearly observed, which corresponded to (100) facet of graphite and (111) facet of Au, which was strong evidence for the formation of PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites. As shown in Fig. 1B, the PAMAM-CDs were attached to the surface of Au nanocrystal. XPS measurements were carried out to probe the surface of the chemical composition of as-prepared PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites (Fig. S4C; D). The results showed that the as prepared PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites contained Au, C, N, and O. Compared with PAMAM-CDs, the Au 4f doublet (83.3eV and 86.9eV), Au 4d3/2 (353eV) and Au 4d5/3 (335eV) were observed, confirming the formation of metallic Au on the PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites. Further EDS element mapping clearly presented the elements of Au, C, and N distributed in the PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites (Fig. S5). These results indicated that as reducing and capping agents, PAMAM-CDs were coated on the surface of Au nanocrystal. However, both the XPS and EDS provided convincing evidence for the formation of Au nanocrystal on the PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites. From the results, we might conclude that the surface of Au crystal was not fully covered by the PAMAM-CDs. In this case, the anti-AFP can be immobilized directly on the uncovered surface of Au nanocrystal. The schematic diagram of the PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites is shown in Scheme 1A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication process of the PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites-modified electrochemical immunosensor is shown in Scheme 1B. PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites can be stably fixed on the surface of GCE by casting. Then, anti-AFP was attached to the nanocomposites via the electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions between Au nanocrystal and primary amine groups of antibodies (Cui et al., 2007). BSA was used to block the possible sites that may cause the nonspecific absorption. Finally, AFP could be immobilized on GCE surface modified with anti-AFP through the immunorecognition, which was expected to attenuate the electrochemical activity and decrease the current of immunosensor. Thus, the concentration of AFP can be quantified. The SEM image of PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites modified electrochemical immunosensor surface is shown in Fig. S6.

The DPV was used to monitor the fabrication process and the performance of the immunosensor. As shown in Fig. 2A, after modification with PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites (curve b), the peak current was a little decreased compared with bare GCE (curve a), which might be due to the dendrimer structure of PAMAM hinder the diffusion of ferricyanide toward the electrode surface (Qian and Yang, 2006). However, owing to the excellent conductivity of CDs and Au nanocrystal, the peak current still remained high. The loading of anti-AFP leaded to a conspicuous decrease of the peak current (curve c) due to the formation of an electron-blocking layer. Subsequently, the current response further decreased after being blocked with BSA (curve d) and incubated in a solution in 1ngmL−1 AFP (curve e), which was ascribed to the insulating protein layers on the electrode retarding the electron transfer.

In order to discuss the roles of CD in this sensor configuration, control experiments have been done. PAMAM–Au was prepared according to the report (Qu et al., 2010). As shown in Fig. S8, the peak current of PAMAM-CDs/Au modified GCE was larger than that of PAMAM–Au modified GCE. This indicated that the CDs could accelerate the electron transfer between sensing interface and the electrode surface due to the excellent conductivity of CDs. When CDs were attached to the surface of Au nanocrystal, the relative position of CDs band edge permits the transfer from the Au nanocrystal surface. The electrons can be shuttled freely along the conducting network of CDs (Li et al., 2010).

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was further been employed to characterize the interface properties of surface-modified immunosensors (Fig. 2B). In the spectra, the semicircle in the high-frequency region and the straight line in the low frequency region correspond to the electron transfer and diffusion process, respectively (Bogomolova et al., 2009). An almost straight line can be observed for bare GCE (curve a). The EIS of PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites showed a larger interfacial electron transfer resistance compared with bare GCE. After anti-AFP was immobilized on the electrode (curve c), the interfacial resistance was remarkably increased, confirming the successful immobilization of anti-AFP. Then a larger semicircle domain can be found, indicating the higher resistance of electrode interface as the immunosensor was blocked with BSA (curve d). The incubation of AFP formed a protein layer to obstruct the electron transfer on the electrode, showing a large semicircle domain of curve e. The impedance spectra were modeled with the Randles equivalent circuit presented in the inset in Fig. 2B, which has been used in several reports of biosensors (Hou et al., 2013; Jeong et al., 2013). The equivalent circuit takes into account impedance properties of bio-modified electrode, represented by the solution resistance R s, electron transfer resistance R et, and double layer capacitance C dl. The impedance experiment results for the process of modified electrode were agreed with the results from DPV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental parameters of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of the detection solution could influence the activity of the protein activity and the antigen–antibody reaction, thus the effect of pH values from 6.5 to 8.5 on the immunosensor performance was investigated in this work. As shown in Fig. S9A, the current response increased from 6.5 to 7.3 and then decreased at higher pH condition. Thus, pH 7.3 was chosen as the optimal value and used throughout the experiment.

The incubation temperature and time are also important factors affecting the analytical performance. As shown, the results showed that the change of current gradually increased as the temperature increased from 15 to 37°C, and then decreased as the temperature increased above 37°C (Fig. S9B). Therefore, 37°C was an optimal temperature for immunoreaction. With the increasing incubation time, the change of current increased and trended to a constant value after an incubation time of 30min (Fig. S9C). As a result, the optimum incubation time was set at 30min in this study.

Under the optimum conditions, the sensitivity and dynamic range of the immunoassay were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 3A, the DPV peak currents decreased with the incremental AFP concentration. The calibration plots showed a linear relationship in the range from 100fgmL−1 to 100ngmL−1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.996. The linear equation was y=−1.314x+20.246, and the detection limit for AFP was 0.025pgmL−1 (S/N=3). Additionally, the analytical performance of this sensor has been compared with other carbon nanomaterials-based label-free electrochemical immunosensors for the detection of AFP (Table S1). As can be seen, the linear range and detection limit of the proposed immunosensor were greatly improved and exhibited a lower detection limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, repeatability, and stability of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity plays an important role in analyzing biological samples. Thus, the potential interferences, including CEA, IgG, BSA, Glu, UA and AA were investigated by incubation of the anti-AFP modified sensing interface with the above solution (Fig. 3B). Compared with the current response obtained from pure AFP, the variation in current caused by the interference substances was less than 5%. Moreover, the control experiment incubating with the interferences without AFP were also conducted. These results showed that the immunosensor possessed excellent selectivity for AFP.

The repeatability of the immunosensors was examined by analysis of the same concentration of AFP (1ngmL−1) using five equally prepared electrodes. The five immunosensors, made independently, exhibited similar current response and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.6% (less than 5%). Therefore, the proposed immunosensor exhibited good repeatability.

Stability of immunosensor is an important factor in their application and development. When the immunosensor was stored at 4°C, it remained 92.6% of its initial response after one week and 83.1% after a storage period of 30 days. These results indicated that the proposed immunosensor represented excellent stability.

The analytical reliability and application potential of the proposed method were evaluated by comparing the assay results of clinical serum samples using the proposed immunosensor with the reference values obtained by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). These clinical human serum samples were from the Capital Normal University Hospital. Each human serum samples were analyzed for three times. The relative deviation between these two methods was from −5.3% to 2.5% ( Table 1). Good agreement between the immunosensor and ELISA was obtained, showing the applicability of the proposed immunosensor in real biological samples.

In summary, this work presented simple approaches to synthesize PAMAM-CDs and PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites. The resulting nanocomposites exhibited excellent conductivity, stability and biocompatibility on the surface of electrode. A simple, label-free and sensitive AFP amperometric immunosensor was fabricated by simply immobilizing anti-AFP on the resulting nanocomposites. This protocol was demonstrated to be simple and showed high sensitivity, good repeatability, satisfied regeneration and wide dynamic response for AFP quantification. The immunosensor was successfully applied for the detection of AFP in human serum sample. In addition, the PAMAM-CDs/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites extended the application of carbon dots in the field of electrochemical immunosensor and provided a new biocompatible platform for further biosensing and biological assays. Moreover, the concept of using carbon dots as building block to immobilize biomolecule can be readily extend to the construction of other novel immunosensor systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financed by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21273153) and the Beijing Natural Science Foundation (2132008).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2013.05.048.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Journal of the American Chemical Society
Journal of the American Chemical Society
